message:
feat(checkpoint): add auto-checkpoint on risky operations (ntm-7zx4)

Implements automatic checkpoint creation before potentially risky operations:
- Auto-checkpoint before broadcast sends (--all or default mode)
- Auto-checkpoint before adding 3+ agents
- Configurable via [checkpoints] section in config.toml
- Rotation policy to limit auto-checkpoints per session

New config options:
- enabled: master toggle for auto-checkpoints
- before_broadcast: checkpoint before all-agent sends
- before_add_agents: threshold for add command
- max_auto_checkpoints: rotation limit per session
- scrollback_lines, include_git: capture options

 {"id":"ntm-0d7","title":"Implement ntm mail ack and read commands","description":"# Implement ntm mail ack and read Commands

## Purpose

Allow marking messages as read or acknowledged, which signals to senders that the message was received and processed.

## Command Syntax

```bash
# Mark message as read
ntm mail read myproject 42

# Mark message as acknowledged
ntm mail ack myproject 42

# Acknowledge all urgent messages
ntm mail ack myproject --urgent

# Acknowledge all from specific agent
ntm mail ack myproject --from cc_1
```

## Semantics

- **Read**: Message was seen but not necessarily acted upon
- **Acknowledged**: Message was seen AND the recipient has taken action or committed to action

Agent Mail tracks both timestamps separately:
- read_ts: When mark_message_read was called
- ack_ts: When acknowledge_message was called

## Batch Operations

```bash
# Mark multiple messages
ntm mail read myproject 42 43 44

# Mark all unread as read
ntm mail read myproject --all

# Acknowledge all pending
ntm mail ack myproject --all
```

## Output

```
✓ Message #42 marked as read
✓ Message #42 acknowledged (ack_ts: 2025-12-10T15:35:00Z)
```

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Single message read/ack by ID
- [ ] Batch operations by ID list
- [ ] Filter-based batch: --urgent, --from, --all
- [ ] Proper error handling for already-read/acked
- [ ] JSON output mode for scripts","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2025-12-10T03:18:49.63059-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-10T03:18:49.63059-05:00","dependencies":[{"issue_id":"ntm-0d7","depends_on_id":"ntm-161","type":"blocks","created_at":"2025-12-10T03:19:56.022722-05:00","created_by":"daemon"}]}

 {"id":"ntm-161","title":"Implement ntm mail inbox command","description":"# Implement ntm mail inbox Command

## Purpose

Display an AGGREGATE inbox view showing messages across ALL agents in the project, not just NTM's coordinator messages.

## IMPORTANT: Aggregate View

This is not just NTM's inbox. It shows:
1. Messages TO any agent in the project (GreenCastle, BlueLake, etc.)
2. Messages FROM any agent in the project
3. Filtered by session agents if desired

This gives the user visibility into all agent-to-agent communication.

## Command Syntax

```bash
# Show all messages in project (across all agents)
ntm mail inbox myproject

# Filter to session's agents only
ntm mail inbox myproject --session-agents

# Filter to specific agent
ntm mail inbox myproject --agent GreenCastle

# Urgent only
ntm mail inbox myproject --urgent

# JSON for scripts
ntm mail inbox myproject --json
```

## Output Format

```
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ Project Inbox: myproject (5 agents)                         │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ ● #42 [URGENT] API Review Needed                            │
│   GreenCastle → BlueLake                                    │
│   2 minutes ago │ Thread: FEAT-123 │ ⚠️ Ack required         │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ ○ #41 Tests passing, ready for merge                        │
│   RedStone → GreenCastle, BlueLake                          │
│   15 minutes ago │ Thread: FEAT-123                         │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ ○ #40 Starting work on src/api/**                           │
│   ntm_myproject → all                                       │
│   30 minutes ago │ (coordinator announcement)               │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
Showing messages involving: GreenCastle, BlueLake, RedStone, ntm_myproject
```

## Implementation

1. Discover all agents in project via Agent Mail
2. Fetch inbox for EACH agent
3. Deduplicate (same message may appear in multiple inboxes)
4. Sort by timestamp
5. Display with sender → recipient format

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Shows messages across ALL project agents
- [ ] Deduplication (message shown once even if multiple recipients)
- [ ] Filter by --session-agents, --agent, --urgent
- [ ] Clear indication of sender → recipient
- [ ] Coordinator messages distinguished from agent messages

 {"id":"ntm-20n","title":"Implement --robot-assign for work distribution recommendations","description":"AI-assisted work assignment suggestions based on current state.

## Rationale
With N agents and M ready beads, optimal assignment is non-trivial. This flag analyzes current state and recommends which agent should work on what, considering:
- Agent types and their strengths
- Current workload balance  
- Bead dependencies and priorities
- Recent activity patterns

## Usage
```bash
ntm --robot-assign myproject
ntm --robot-assign myproject --beads=ntm-abc,ntm-def  # specific beads
ntm --robot-assign myproject --strategy=balanced      # or: speed, quality
```

## Output Structure
```json
{
  "session": "myproject",
  "strategy": "balanced",
  "recommendations": [
    {
      "agent": "0.1",
      "agent_type": "claude",
      "assign_bead": "ntm-1rz",
      "confidence": 0.85,
      "reasoning": "Profiler task matches claude's code analysis strengths"
    },
    {
      "agent": "0.2", 
      "agent_type": "codex",
      "assign_bead": "ntm-7mg",
      "confidence": 0.72,
      "reasoning": "UI/styling task, codex handles CSS well"
    }
  ],
  "blocked_beads": [
    {"id": "ntm-xyz", "blocked_by": ["ntm-abc"]}
  ],
  "idle_agents": ["0.3"],
  "unassignable_beads": [
    {"id": "ntm-qqq", "reason": "Requires human input"}
  ]
}
```

## Assignment Strategies
- balanced: distribute work evenly across agents
- speed: assign to fastest/most active agents  
- quality: match task types to agent strengths
- dependency: prioritize unblocking work

## Integration with Beads
Requires parsing bd ready and bd show output to understand task graph. Consider caching bead state to avoid repeated queries.

## Future Enhancement
Could integrate with agent-mail to check agent's stated preferences or current focus.","status":"in_progress","priority":1,"issue_type":"task","assignee":"RedCat","created_at":"2025-12-10T02:51:50.71632-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-10T04:21:29.033695-05:00"}
 {"id":"ntm-22f","title":"Implement pre/post command hooks","description":"Run user scripts before and after NTM commands.

## What This Task Does

Enable users to define hooks that run before or after specific
NTM commands. Hooks can log, notify, validate, or transform.

## Configuration

# ~/.config/ntm/config.toml

[hooks]
# Run before any send command
pre_send = "~/.config/ntm/hooks/log-prompt.sh"

# Run after spawn completes
post_spawn = "notify-send 'NTM' 'Session created'"

# Run before dangerous commands
pre_kill = "~/.config/ntm/hooks/confirm-kill.sh"

## Hook Interface

Hooks receive command context as JSON on stdin:
{
  "command": "send",
  "args": ["myproject", "--all", "hello"],
  "session": "myproject",
  "flags": {"all": true},
  "timestamp": "2025-12-10T10:00:00Z"
}

Post hooks also receive result:
{
  "command": "spawn",
  "result": "success",
  "session": "myproject",
  "panes_created": 4,
  "duration_ms": 1234
}

## Hook Execution

Pre hooks:
- Run synchronously before command
- Non-zero exit blocks the command
- Stdout captured but not displayed (log only)
- Stderr shown to user

Post hooks:
- Run asynchronously after command
- Exit code doesn't affect NTM
- Good for notifications, logging

## Disabling Hooks

For scripting or sensitive operations:
$ ntm send --no-hooks myproject 'message'

## Example Hooks

Log all prompts:
#\!/bin/bash
jq -c '{time: .timestamp, session: .session, prompt: .args[2]}' >> ~/.ntm-prompts.jsonl

Confirm before kill:
#\!/bin/bash
session=$(jq -r '.session' -)
read -p "Really kill $session? [y/N] " confirm
[[ "$confirm" == "y" ]] || exit 1

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Hooks defined in config
- [ ] Pre hooks run before command
- [ ] Pre hook failure blocks command
- [ ] Post hooks run after command
- [ ] Command context passed as JSON
- [ ] --no-hooks flag disables hooks

-{"id":"ntm-239","title":"Implement ntm health command for agent status checking","description":"Create a command to check health status of all agents in a session.

## What This Task Does

Create 'ntm health' command that checks and reports agent health:
- Process status (running/exited)
- Activity level (active/idle/stale)
- Error detection (rate limits, crashes)
- Output summary with visual indicators

## Command Interface

$ ntm health myproject
Session: myproject
┌────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ Pane │ Agent   │ Status  │ Activity │ Issues  │
├────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ 0    │ cc_1    │ ✓ OK    │ active   │ -       │
│ 1    │ cc_2    │ ✓ OK    │ idle     │ -       │
│ 2    │ cod_1   │ ⚠ WARN  │ stale    │ no output 5m │
│ 3    │ gmi_1   │ ✗ ERROR │ crashed  │ exit 1  │
└────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

Overall: 2 healthy, 1 warning, 1 error

$ ntm health myproject --json
{
  "session": "myproject",
  "checked_at": "2025-12-10T10:00:00Z",
  "agents": [...],
  "summary": {"healthy": 2, "warning": 1, "error": 1}
}

## Health Detection Logic

Process status:
- Check if pane has running process (tmux list-panes -F '#{pane_pid}')
- Check process state via /proc or ps

Activity detection:
- Capture pane content, compare over time
- 'active': Content changed recently (< 30s)
- 'idle': No change, but process running (prompt visible)
- 'stale': No change for extended period (> 5m)

Error detection (regex patterns):
- Rate limit: 'rate limit', '429', 'too many requests'
- Auth error: 'authentication', 'unauthorized', '401'
- Crash: 'error', 'exception', 'panic', exit codes

## Implementation

1. Create internal/health package
2. Implement process status checker
3. Implement activity detector
4. Implement error pattern matcher
5. Create ntm health command with TUI output
6. Add --json support (depends on ntm-ws2)

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] ntm health shows all agents with status
- [ ] Process crashes are detected
- [ ] Rate limit patterns are detected
- [ ] Activity level shown (active/idle/stale)
- [ ] Exit code reflects overall health
- [ ] --json output supported

 {"id":"ntm-2e9","title":"Implement stdin and --file support for ntm send","description":"Enable sending prompts from files and stdin pipes.

## What This Task Does

Extend ntm send to accept prompt content from:
1. --file flag: Read prompt from a file
2. stdin: When piped/redirected and no prompt argument given

## Use Cases

From file:
$ ntm send myproject --all --file prompts/review.md

From stdin (pipe):
$ cat error.log | ntm send myproject --cc 'Analyze this error:'
$ git diff | ntm send myproject --all 'Review these changes'

From stdin (redirect):
$ ntm send myproject --all < prompt.txt

Combined with prefix:
$ cat code.py | ntm send myproject --cc --prefix 'Review this code:\n'

## Technical Details

Detection logic:
1. If --file specified, read from file
2. Else if stdin is not a TTY, read from stdin
3. Else use positional prompt argument

Implementation:
func getPromptContent(cmd *cobra.Command, args []string) (string, error) {
    if file != "" {
        content, err := os.ReadFile(file)
        return string(content), err
    }
    
    if !isatty.IsTerminal(os.Stdin.Fd()) {
        content, err := io.ReadAll(os.Stdin)
        return string(content), err
    }
    
    if len(args) > 1 {
        return args[1], nil
    }
    
    return "", errors.New("no prompt provided")
}

New flags:
--file, -f    Read prompt from file
--prefix      Text to prepend to file/stdin content
--suffix      Text to append to file/stdin content

## Edge Cases

- Empty file: Error with helpful message
- Binary file: Warning, but proceed if forced
- Very large file: Stream rather than load all into memory? (future)
- Encoding: Assume UTF-8

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] --file reads prompt from specified path
- [ ] Stdin detected and read when not TTY

 {"id":"ntm-7nk3","title":"EPIC: Checkpoint & Rollback System","description":"# Checkpoint & Rollback System

## Background & Motivation

AI agents sometimes go off track. By the time users notice, significant work may be wasted.

Checkpoints provide quick recovery:
1. Create named save points before risky operations
2. Roll back to known-good state when things go wrong

## What Gets Checkpointed

### Captured (for restoration)
- **Git state**: Uncommitted changes saved as patch
- **Session layout**: Pane configuration, agent types

### Captured (for reference only)
- **Pane scrollback**: What agents were doing (NOT restored)
- **Timestamp and description**: Human context

### NOT Captured
- Agent internal state (we can't access it)
- API conversation history (we can't access it)

## IMPORTANT: What Rollback Does

Rollback restores GIT STATE, not agent state.

```bash
ntm rollback myproject "before-refactor"
```

This will:
1. ✅ Stash current uncommitted changes
2. ✅ Apply checkpoint's git patch
3. ✅ Interrupt all agents (Ctrl+C)
4. ⚠️ Agents restart with FRESH context (no memory of pre-rollback work)

**Critical understanding**: Agents don't "remember" the checkpoint. They start fresh. The checkpoint restores FILES, not agent memory.

## Command Interface

```bash
# Create checkpoint
ntm checkpoint myproject "before-refactor"
# Captures: git diff, session layout, scrollback (for reference)

# List checkpoints
ntm checkpoints myproject
┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│ Checkpoints: myproject                                      │
├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
│ before-refactor    │ 15 min ago │ 3 files │ 4 agents      │
│ api-working        │ 2 hrs ago  │ 1 file  │ 4 agents      │
│ auto-20251210-1400 │ 3 hrs ago  │ 8 files │ 4 agents      │
└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

# Preview rollback
ntm rollback myproject "before-refactor" --dry-run

# Execute rollback
ntm rollback myproject "before-refactor"
# Warning: This will reset files and interrupt agents.
# Current changes will be stashed. Continue? [y/N]

# Quick rollback to last checkpoint
ntm rollback myproject --last
```

## Storage Format

```
~/.config/ntm/checkpoints/myproject/before-refactor/
├── metadata.json         # Timestamp, description, session info
├── git-state.patch       # git diff output
├── session-layout.json   # Pane configuration
└── scrollback/           # Reference only (not restored)
    ├── cc_1.txt
    └── cod_1.txt
```

## Auto-Checkpoints

Configurable automatic checkpoints:
```toml
[checkpoints]
auto_enabled = true
auto_events = ["broadcast_send"]  # Before ntm send --all
max_auto_checkpoints = 10
retention_days = 7
```

## Validation

Before rollback:
- Check if checkpoint exists
- Warn if checkpoint is old (>24h)
- Warn if git state has diverged significantly
- Validate patch can apply

## Success Criteria

- [ ] ntm checkpoint creates named checkpoints
- [ ] Git state (uncommitted) captured as patch
- [ ] Session layout captured for reference
- [ ] Scrollback captured for reference (not restored)
- [ ] ntm rollback restores git state
- [ ] Agents interrupted on rollback (fresh start)
- [ ] Auto-checkpoint on configurable events
- [ ] Retention policy with rotation
- [ ] Clear user messaging about what rollback does/doesn't do","status":"open","priority":1,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2025-12-10T03:28:29.179411-05:00","updated_at":"2025-12-10T03:36:34.909996-05:00"}

 {"id":"ntm-84t","title":"Implement auto-restart for crashed agents","description":"Automatically restart agents that crash or exit unexpectedly.

## What This Task Does

Add --auto-restart flag to ntm spawn that monitors agents and restarts
them if they crash. This builds on the health check infrastructure.

## Interface

$ ntm spawn myproject --cc=2 --auto-restart
Starting agents with auto-restart enabled...
[Agent cc_1 crashed, restarting in 30s...]
[Agent cc_1 restarted successfully]

Configuration:
[resilience]
auto_restart = true
max_restarts = 3
restart_delay_seconds = 30

## Technical Approach

Option 1: Background goroutine
- Spawn launches a monitor goroutine
- Periodically checks health
- Restarts crashed agents
- Exits when session ends

Option 2: Separate daemon
- ntm spawn --auto-restart starts a background process
- Process monitors and restarts
- Survives terminal close

Recommendation: Start with Option 1 (simpler), evolve to Option 2 if needed.

## Restart Logic

1. Detect crash via health check (exit code != 0, process gone)
2. Check restart count for this agent
3. If under max_restarts:
   a. Wait restart_delay_seconds
   b. Get agent type from pane title
   c. Re-run agent command in same pane
   d. Increment restart count
4. If over max_restarts:
   a. Log failure
   b. Send notification (if enabled)
   c. Stop monitoring this agent

## State Tracking

Need to track per-agent:
- Restart count since session start
- Last restart time
- Original agent command

Store in:
- Memory (simple, lost on ntm exit)
- Tmux environment variable (persists)
- File (overkill for now)

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] --auto-restart flag enables monitoring
- [ ] Crashed agents are detected and restarted
- [ ] Restart delay is configurable
- [ ] Max restart limit is enforced
- [ ] Restart count shown in health output
- [ ] Works with existing spawn command

 {"id":"ntm-ozcj.7","title":"CASS + MCP Agent Mail: Cross-agent knowledge sharing","description":"Integrate CASS with MCP Agent Mail for intelligent cross-agent coordination.

## Overview
Enable agents to check CASS before starting work and share findings via Agent Mail, creating a knowledge-sharing loop.

## Use Cases

### Before Starting Work
When agent claims a task:
1. Query CASS for similar past work
2. If found, include in agent context
3. Agent can decide to reuse vs. re-implement

### After Completing Work
When agent finishes:
1. New session auto-indexed by CASS
2. Summary posted to Agent Mail thread
3. Other agents can reference it

### Cross-Project Knowledge
When working on similar problems across projects:
1. Query CASS without workspace filter
2. Find solutions from other projects
3. Adapt patterns (not copy code)

## Implementation

### Agent Mail Integration
```go
// Before claiming task
func (c *Coordinator) CheckCASSBeforeWork(task string) (*CASSContext, error) {
    results, err := c.cass.Search(task, &SearchOptions{
        Limit: 5,
        Since: "30d",
    })
    if err != nil {
        return nil, err
    }
    
    if len(results.Hits) > 0 {
        return &CASSContext{
            RelevantSessions: results.Hits,
            Suggestion: "Review these before re-implementing",
        }, nil
    }
    return nil, nil
}

// After completing work
func (c *Coordinator) NotifyCompletionWithCASSRef(taskID, sessionPath string) error {
    // Post to Agent Mail with CASS reference
    return c.mail.SendMessage(Message{
        Subject: fmt.Sprintf("Completed: %s", taskID),
        Body: fmt.Sprintf("Work completed. Session indexed in CASS: %s", sessionPath),
        ThreadID: taskID,
    })
}
```

### Workflow Example
```
1. Agent claims beads task "implement rate limiting"
2. NTM queries CASS: "rate limiting" in last 30 days
3. Found: Session from 2 weeks ago implementing rate limiting
4. Agent receives context: "Past implementation used token bucket..."
5. Agent adapts pattern, completes faster
6. Posts to Agent Mail: "Completed. Improved on past approach."
7. New session indexed, available for future agents
```

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] Agents receive CASS context when claiming tasks
- [ ] Completed work notified via Agent Mail
- [ ] Cross-project search works
- [ ] Integration with beads workflow

## Rationale
Sometimes I need to redirect an agent urgently - stop what you're doing, do this instead. Regular send just queues; interrupt needs to actually break the agent's current flow.

## Usage

1. Send Ctrl+C to break current operation
2. Wait for prompt/ready state
3. Send the interrupt message

## Options
- --force: Send Ctrl+C even if agent appears idle
- --no-wait: Don't wait for ready state
- --preserve-context: Try to save agent's context before interrupting

    // ... existing setup ...

            // Don't break - continue to other panes
        }
    }
    
    // Complete entry
    entry.Targets = targets
    entry.Success = sendErr == nil
    if sendErr \!= nil {
        entry.Error = sendErr.Error()
    }
    
    // Log to history (ignore errors - don't fail send for history)
    if err := history.Append(entry); err \!= nil {

}

## Edit Mode
When --edit is specified:
1. Write prompt to temp file
2. Open in 
3. Read modified content
4. Confirm and send

```go
func editPrompt(original string) (string, error) {
    f, _ := os.CreateTemp("", "ntm-prompt-*.md")
    f.WriteString(original)
    f.Close()
    
    editor := os.Getenv("EDITOR")
    if editor == "" { editor = "vim" }
    
    cmd := exec.Command(editor, f.Name())
    cmd.Stdin = os.Stdin
    cmd.Stdout = os.Stdout
    cmd.Run()
    
    modified, _ := os.ReadFile(f.Name())
    return string(modified), nil
}
```

 {"id":"ntm-pp5.3","title":"Prompt Templates with Variables","description":"Named templates with variable substitution for reusable prompts.

## Overview
Templates are named, parameterized prompts stored in files.
Users can invoke them via `ntm send --template=name`.

## Storage Locations
1. Global: ~/.config/ntm/templates/*.md
2. Project: .ntm/templates/*.md (if Per-Project Config enabled)

## Template Format
```markdown
---
name: code_review
description: Review code for quality issues
variables:
  file: File path to review
  focus: What to focus on (optional)
---

Review the file {{file}} for:
- Code quality issues
- Potential bugs
- Performance concerns
{{#focus}}
Focus especially on: {{focus}}
{{/focus}}

Be thorough but concise.
```

## Variable Types

### Required Variables
`{{variable}}` - Must be provided via --var

### Optional Variables (with conditionals)
`{{#variable}}...{{/variable}}` - Section only included if variable provided

### Built-in Variables
| Variable | Value |
|----------|-------|
| `{{file}}` | Content of --file argument |
| `{{files}}` | Content of all --file arguments |
| `{{selection}}` | Current editor selection (if available) |
| `{{clipboard}}` | Clipboard content |
| `{{cwd}}` | Current working directory |
| `{{session}}` | Session name |
| `{{date}}` | Current date |
| `{{time}}` | Current time |

## Command Interface
```bash
# Use template
ntm send myproject --template=code_review --var file=src/auth.py

# Use with file injection
ntm send myproject -t code_review --file=src/auth.py
# {{file}} auto-populated with file contents

# List available templates
ntm template list

# Show template content
ntm template show code_review

# Create new template
ntm template create my_template
# Opens editor with scaffold
```

## Template Resolution
1. Look in project templates (.ntm/templates/)
2. Look in global templates (~/.config/ntm/templates/)
3. Look in built-in templates (embedded in binary)

## Built-in Templates
Ship with sensible defaults:
- code_review: Review code for issues
- explain: Explain how code works
- refactor: Refactor code
- test: Write tests
- document: Add documentation

## Parsing Implementation
```go
func ParseTemplate(content string, vars map[string]string) (string, error) {
    // Parse frontmatter
    meta, body := splitFrontmatter(content)
    
    // Check required variables
    for _, v := range meta.Variables {
        if _, ok := vars[v.Name]; \!ok && v.Required {
            return "", fmt.Errorf("missing required variable: %s", v.Name)
        }
    }
    
    // Expand conditionals
    body = expandConditionals(body, vars)
    
    // Substitute variables
    body = substituteVariables(body, vars)
    
    return body, nil
}
```

## What This Task Does

Enable users to create custom NTM commands by placing executable
scripts in ~/.config/ntm/commands/. Any executable becomes a subcommand.

## Plugin Location

~/.config/ntm/commands/
├── sync-all*         # ntm sync-all
├── morning-setup*    # ntm morning-setup  
└── deploy*           # ntm deploy

## Script Interface

Scripts receive NTM context via environment variables:
- NTM_SESSION: Current/specified session name
- NTM_JSON: Set to '1' if --json flag passed
- NTM_CONFIG_PATH: Path to NTM config file
- NTM_VERSION: NTM version string

Scripts can use ntm commands internally:
#\!/bin/bash
# ~/.config/ntm/commands/sync-all
for session in $(ntm list --json | jq -r '.[].name'); do
  ntm send "$session" --all "git pull && npm install"
done

## Command Discovery

On startup:
1. Scan ~/.config/ntm/commands/
2. Check each file is executable
3. Register as cobra subcommand
4. Extract description from script header comment

Script description convention:
#\!/bin/bash
# Description: Sync all agents with latest code
# Usage: ntm sync-all [--force]

...
Plugin Commands:

  morning-setup Set up morning development session
  deploy        Deploy current branch to staging

$ ntm sync-all --help
# Runs: script --help

Pass through all arguments to script:
$ ntm deploy --env staging --force

- [ ] Executables in commands/ become subcommands

 {"id":"ntm-yxa","title":"Implement session agent registration","description":"# Implement Session Coordinator Registration

## Purpose

Register NTM as a COORDINATOR agent with Agent Mail - not as a replacement for the actual agents, but as the orchestration layer.

## IMPORTANT CLARIFICATION

NTM registers as a **coordinator**, not as the agents themselves:
- Identity: `ntm_<session>` (e.g., `ntm_myproject`)
- Program: `ntm`
- Role: Coordinator/orchestrator
- Purpose: User-initiated messages, session-level file reservations

The actual Claude/Codex/Gemini agents may have their own Agent Mail identities (e.g., `GreenCastle`, `BlueLake`). NTM doesn't impersonate them.

## Registration Flow

```go
func RegisterSessionCoordinator(client *agentmail.Client, sessionName, projectDir string) (*agentmail.Agent, error) {
    // 1. Ensure project exists
    if _, err := client.EnsureProject(projectDir); err != nil {
        return nil, err
    }
    
    // 2. Register coordinator identity
    return client.RegisterAgent(RegisterAgentInput{
        ProjectKey:      projectDir,
        Program:         "ntm",
        Model:           "coordinator",
        Name:            fmt.Sprintf("ntm_%s", sanitize(sessionName)),
        TaskDescription: "NTM session coordinator - user-initiated actions",
    })
}
```

## When Registration Happens

- **On spawn**: Register coordinator after session creation
- **On attach**: Update last_active_ts (agent already exists)
- **Optional**: Can skip if Agent Mail unavailable

## Discovering Other Agents

After registration, NTM can discover other agents in the project:
```go
agents, _ := client.ListAgents(projectDir)
// Returns: GreenCastle (claude-code), BlueLake (claude-code), RedStone (codex-cli), etc.
```

This enables the aggregate inbox view.

- [ ] Coordinator registered as `ntm_<session>` on spawn
- [ ] Registration is idempotent (attach doesn't create duplicate)
- [ ] Other project agents discoverable via ListAgents

-{"id":"ntm-z2k","title":"Update pane naming to include model identifier","description":"# Update Pane Naming to Include Variant Identifier

## Purpose

Enhance pane titles to include model OR persona information for identification and filtering.

## CRITICAL: Resolving Naming Conflict

Both Multi-Model and Personas want to add info to pane names. We need ONE consistent scheme.

**Decision: Use "variant" as a unified concept**
- Variant = persona name (if persona specified)
- Variant = model alias (if only model specified)
- Variant = empty (if neither, for backwards compatibility)

## Naming Format

`{session}__{type}_{index}[_{variant}]`

Examples:
```
myproject__cc_1              # No variant (default model, no persona)
myproject__cc_1_opus         # Model variant (--cc=1:opus)
myproject__cc_1_architect    # Persona variant (--persona=architect)
myproject__cod_2_gpt4        # Model variant
myproject__gmi_1_reviewer    # Persona variant
```

## Priority Rules

1. If --persona specified: Use persona name as variant
2. Else if model specified: Use model alias as variant
3. Else: No variant (backwards compatible)

Rationale: Persona implies model (persona includes model setting), so persona takes precedence.

## Parsing Logic

```go
// Regex: session__type_index[_variant]
var paneNameRegex = regexp.MustCompile(`^(.+)__(\w+)_(\d+)(?:_(\w+))?$`)

type PaneInfo struct {
    Session string
    Type    AgentType  // cc, cod, gmi
    Index   int
    Variant string     // model alias OR persona name, may be empty
}

func ParsePaneName(title string) (*PaneInfo, error) {
    matches := paneNameRegex.FindStringSubmatch(title)
    if matches == nil {
        return nil, ErrInvalidPaneName
    }
    return &PaneInfo{
        Session: matches[1],
        Type:    AgentType(matches[2]),
        Index:   parseInt(matches[3]),
        Variant: matches[4], // May be empty string
    }, nil
}
```

## Variant Type Detection

To know if variant is persona or model:
```go
func (p *PaneInfo) VariantType() VariantType {
    if p.Variant == "" {
        return VariantNone
    }
    if cfg.IsPersonaName(p.Variant) {
        return VariantPersona
    }
    return VariantModel
}
```

## Filtering Support

```bash
ntm send myproject --cc:opus "..."      # Filter by model
ntm send myproject --cc:architect "..." # Filter by persona
ntm send myproject --cc "..."           # All Claude agents
```

 {"id":"ntm-zh1e","title":"Add scanner configuration system (.ntm.yaml)","description":"## Overview
Create a configuration system for UBS scanner integration, allowing customization of scan behavior, thresholds, and tool selection.

## Background
Different projects have different needs - some want strict scanning, others more lenient. A configuration system enables:
- Per-project customization
- Consistent behavior across team
- Easy toggling of features

## Implementation Details

### Config File Location
Search order:
1. .ntm.yaml in project root
2. ~/.config/ntm/config.yaml (global defaults)
3. Built-in defaults

### Config Schema
```yaml
scanner:
  # UBS executable path (auto-detected if not set)
  ubs_path: ~/projects/ultimate_bug_scanner/ubs
  
  # Default scan settings
  defaults:
    timeout: 60s
    parallel: true
    exclude:
      - vendor/**
      - node_modules/**
      - "*.test.go"
  
  # Severity thresholds for different contexts
  thresholds:
    pre_commit:
      block_critical: true
      block_errors: 5
    ci:
      fail_critical: true
      fail_errors: 0
    dashboard:
      show_warnings: true
      show_info: false
  
  # Tool selection (which UBS tools to run)
  tools:
    enabled:
      - semgrep
      - gosec
      - staticcheck
    disabled:
      - bandit  # Python not used in this project
  
  # Auto-bead creation settings
  beads:
    auto_create: true
    min_severity: error
    auto_close: true
    
  # Notification settings
  notifications:
    enabled: true
    on_new_critical: true
    summary_after_scan: true
```

### Files to Create/Modify
- internal/config/scanner.go - Scanner config types
- internal/config/loader.go - Config loading logic
- internal/scanner/config.go - Config integration
